speaker: interviewer
text: Tape six. I'm with Dr. Fred Katz. I'm Lucy Samorodin, the interviewer. Dr. Katz, I asked you if there was anything else you would like to say.

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I feel it's a privilege to be included in this program. I also hope that it will add something to it in addition to simply another historical recollection of what somebody experienced. I hope it will prod people to be creative and innovative and not too scared about approaching this material.

speaker: interviewer
text: Why are you doing your writings now in-- with the Holocaust as your subject?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I feel that the-- it can contribute to a more effective social psychology of human behavior. I'm not particularly interested in explaining the Holocaust more effectively. I'm trying to help create a more effective science of psychology or science of social psychology that can be of service to us, to human beings in a variety of ways so we don't always start out with being surprised when horrendous things happen. So we can-- we can get more control over our destiny. So my s-- my wish, hope is to contribute to a more effective science of human behavior through this work.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you often think about the Shoah?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. Well, I, I would say-- perhaps my friends might disagree with me on that one. I don't think I'm often thinking about it. I do think about my parents. It seems to come in waves. Sometimes I think about my brother, sometimes about my mother and father. [COUGHS] [DRINKS] But I think more about the challenge which this entails. Rather, I want to get away from the victimhood syndrome. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I don't think I think about the Holocaust often, although I'm more at home with being associated with this issue. But I also feel as long as we-- as the Holocaust as such becomes such a central thing, we are debilitating ourselves intellectually and scientifically. We cannot afford to, to rest with this as a unique issue. It is unique as an opportunity to learn something about human behavior.

speaker: interviewer
text: What made you want to tell your story now?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I want to make a contribution to what I was just saying, to, to opening up and loosening up things. And I think the, the Spielberg Project is to be commended in trying to get the records down, but I also feel it is up to us to contribute-- those of us who do contribute our stories-- to show that one can move beyond the Holocaust and do so in a healthy way that will be-- yield benefits to people way beyond ourselves and our loved ones. I want someone to honor my parents and my brother and the people who helped me along the way.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you so much for sharing your story.

speaker: interviewee
text: OK. Thank you. These are my parents, and as far as I know, this is their wedding picture, which would have been taken around 1922. My mother, incidentally, was the nurse that my father hired to take care of his first son when his wife died in giving birth to that son. My mother was the nurse. So this is Jenny, Shenny on the left, and the father's name is Max, Max Katz. Mother's maiden name was Gruenebaum. Our house, the house where I was born, is so on the left. And the, the lower right part is where the butcher shop was. And the family living quarters were on the left and upstairs. The house in the middle was the house of the baker where we spent Kristallnacht hiding in the attic. This picture was given to be by Ernestine during my visit to Oberlauringen. Ernestine is the person in the second row, the second person on the left. At the-- in the first row, the person on the left is the girl, Margaret, who was my playmate and who was killed. My brother is next to her. My brother Ludwig is next to her. In the very back on the right is the father of Margaret who committed suicide. And he is the man I couldn't recognize when he came back from concentration camp. This is my grandfather, the person on the right, and with two of his grandchildren, my cousins. His name was-- it's from my mother's side. His name was Gruenebaum, but I don't remember his first name. He was some sort of Jewish teacher, Hebrew teacher. I remember seeing him once, and he died in the mid '30s. This is a picture of my school class. The tall lady on the right is the school teacher. As far as I know, I am in the front row, the second person on the left. It was sort of all scrunched up. That's me. I also want to talk about the person in the middle with the braids-- so the wide mouth, yes, yes. You're getting her. She was a Jewish girl. When I went-- her name was [? Francie ?] [? Schloss. ?] When I went to visit my village for the one day in 1974, the school-- the people who showed me around told me that after the war ended in 1945, [? Franzik-- ?] Francie and her family had left, had managed to escape. Francie came back with a camera, and she took pictures, and she talked to no one. They remembered this 30 years later that she talked to no one. These are the only items that I brought with me from home from Germany, a watch and two silver spoons. The watch had originally belonged to Emil, my, my mother's brother, who died as a result of wounds from the First World War. He died, I believe, in 1925. And I was named after him. My middle name is Emil. The two spoons are gifts from my uncle, my godfather, who was giving me a silver spoon on each birthday, but these are the only ones I still have. And these are the only items, the physical items, that I still have from home, in addition to two prayer books that I will show in a moment. This is a Hebrew prayer book that I brought with me from home. I think it was published around 1909, and the front pages were used by my mother to record dates and occurrences. This, this one now is my birth date that she recorded, and that's her handwriting. So the births on this side, and on the other side are deaths that she recorded. So the line talks about her brother Emil dying on the 25th of June, and then her other brother dying on when he died. And then underneath I wrote when my parents died, and so I kept up the same tradition of recording these dates in this prayer book. This is the other book I brought with me. It's a Jewish Bible in German, in German language from which I learned Jewish history. This is the only picture I have of my whole family. I believe it was taken around 1937. My mother is on the left in the first row, and I'm in the middle. My father's on the right. And in the back of my sister with the braids is on the left. Trude is my sister's name. My brother's on the right, Ludwig, my father, Max, and my mother, Shenny. My father looks very severe and, and heavy. I don't recall him as such, but he looked pretty severe in those pictures. But this is the only picture I have. This is the house of [? Herta ?] [? Carger ?] in England, 55 Greencroft Gardens in London where I spent many of a Sunday and where a lot was contributed to my life and to many others. This is [? Herta ?] [? Carger ?] who was a very profound influence in my life. My small repayment to her was to dedicate my first book to her. She died about 10 years ago. This is me when I was about 16 or 17 years old-- that is 1945, '44 in England. I was, perhaps, 17. During my first academic job in Lubbock, Texas in 1960, I bought a grave plot in memory of my parents, and I put on it a grave marker for my parents and my brother. I have never again visited there, to my chagrin, but also, it didn't occur to me at that time that this was a Christian cemetery, and my orthodox parents would have been very pained to be buried in a Christian cemetery. But this was my period of denial of anything Jewish, and I saw nothing wrong with it at that time. I do now. This picture was taken near Guilford College in North Carolina around 1950. And the lady on the right in the polka dot dress is Adda Field, who was a very thin little lady, but a very strong woman. Oh, I'm in the middle there-- the-- in the middle in the second row. But these are the three books of mine that was fairly traditional sociology books in the sense that I didn't address the Holocaust at that time. But they represent sort of the work I was doing in the early part of my academic career. This is my first book on the Holocaust related issues on the work that I'm currently doing. I had written articles before. And this one came out in 1993. At present, I have completed a book called that's a sequel to this one called and I'm working on another one called
